target.title: fix(codex): wait for turn completion after post-tool handoff
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes premature Codex Harness turn completion after a tool handoff.

## What Problem This Solves

After a Codex turn has crossed a tool handoff, an assistant `item/completed` notification is not a reliable final-answer boundary. The model may still be continuing the same Codex turn, and the correct release signal is `turn/completed`.

This was an item-level versus turn-level lifecycle mismatch in the native Codex app-server runtime plugin:

- Before tool handoff, a completed assistant item can release the turn.
- After tool handoff, completed assistant items can be intermediate progress/narration while the same turn continues.
- Codex ACP does not hit the same failure mode because its collector finishes on the turn-level completion boundary.

## Changes

- `isAssistantCompletionReleaseNotification(...)` now returns false once the turn has crossed a tool handoff.
- Existing post-tool wait behavior is updated to complete only after `turn/completed`.
- Adds one regression test for the failing lifecycle: post-tool assistant item completion without `turn/completed` must not release the run.

The PR is intentionally limited to 2 files and 1 commit.

## Evidence

Current PR head: `43fa4b02`.

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts -t "does not release post-tool assistant items without turn completion|keeps waiting when a current-turn item is still active"` -> 1 file passed, 2 tests passed, 63 skipped.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.



## Proof Update (2026-06-29)

Current head after docs update: `cdaeef34`.

Validation run:

```text
cd /tmp/openclaw-pr97153
pnpm test -- --run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       65 passed (65)
```

The current-head regression coverage includes `does not release post-tool assistant items without turn completion` in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts`. That scenario emits a post-tool assistant item completion and verifies OpenClaw does not resolve the Codex app-server attempt at the assistant-item boundary; it waits for `turn/completed` or the post-tool idle guard instead.

Docs were added to both Codex Harness docs pages:

- `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md`
- `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`

The new docs explicitly state that after a tool handoff, completed assistant items are current-turn progress, not a final release boundary, and OpenClaw waits for `turn/completed` or the post-tool idle guard. This is the compatibility-sensitive behavior change maintainers need to accept.



## Real Runtime Proof Update (2026-06-29)

Added redacted terminal proof from a real current-head Codex app-server attempt runtime path. This was run locally with a temporary proof-only test file and then removed; it is not part of the PR diff.

The proof used the real `runCodexAppServerAttempt` path with a mocked Codex app-server client, emitted the same event order that caused the bug, and verified OpenClaw did not release before `turn/completed`:

1. `item/completed` for `custom_tool_call_output` marks that the turn crossed a tool handoff.
2. `item/completed` for a post-tool `agentMessage` with text `Now I will verify the generated files.`
3. Waited briefly and checked whether the attempt settled before `turn/completed`.
4. Sent `turn/completed` and let the attempt finish.

Terminal output:

```text
pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/post-tool-boundary-proof.test.ts \
  --reporter verbose --silent=false

stderr | extensions/codex/src/app-server/post-tool-boundary-proof.test.ts > post-tool boundary runtime proof > does not release on post-tool assistant item before turn/completed
{
  "settledBeforeTurnCompleted": false,
  "finalText": "Now I will verify the generated files.",
  "turnInterruptCalls": 0
}

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       1 passed (1)
```

This directly proves the current-head app-server behavior: after a tool handoff, the post-tool assistant item is treated as progress, not as a release boundary. The attempt stayed open until `turn/completed`, and no interrupt fallback was needed.

target.title: fix compaction timeout partial progress
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve completed chunk summaries when a later compaction chunk times out, so timeout recovery can reuse partial progress instead of falling back to a generic “summary unavailable” note and restarting from chunk 1 on the next attempt.
- Preserve cooperative partial progress through the outer `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` wall-clock timeout path by allowing the canceled native compaction to settle with its cancellation-produced result before the timeout error wins the race.
- Wire the safety-timeout reason through the real native `activeSession.compact()` / `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` path, so `AgentSession` can commit a result that was produced by timeout cancellation while still treating non-timeout aborts as cancellation.
- Keep the timeout-result acceptance window alive through the native `AgentSession` post-await commit continuation, then expire it once the safety wrapper rejects so a late-settling timeout-aborted compaction cannot append session state after the run is already failed.
- Remove the old `src/node-host/with-timeout.ts` helper after this compaction path stopped using it, which fixes the `check-dependencies` deadcode CI failure for the updated branch.
- Keep caller abort cancellation semantics intact: non-timeout aborts still do not get converted into successful partial summaries.
- Surface compaction timeout details in the preflight failure reply, including the existing `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds` knob, while generic non-verbose compaction failures still hide details.

Fixes #92043

## Maintainer-ready notes

- **Why it matters / User impact:** Users with large transcripts can hit the default compaction wall-clock timeout after some chunks already summarized successfully. Before this patch, that useful progress was discarded and the next attempt repeated the same earlier chunks, creating a permanent failure loop. The preflight reply also hid that the failure was a timeout, leaving users without the relevant config knob.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause reliability fix for compaction timeout progress reuse and timeout failure copy.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the local source/runtime surface covered here. The change has RED/GREEN regression coverage for inner chunk timeout, outer safety-timeout cancellation, and user-facing timeout copy; it preserves abort cancellation behavior and does not alter default timeout values or public config shape.
- **Root cause:** The root source was split across three timeout boundaries. `summarizeChunks()` discarded completed chunk summaries on timeout-shaped failures; the outer `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` could reject with its wall-clock timeout before a cooperating native compaction returned the partial result it produced during timeout cancellation; the real native `AgentSession` path aborted compaction without preserving the timeout reason, so a result produced after timeout cancellation was still classified as plain aborted work; the timeout-abort classification originally accepted any matching error name, which left a late-settling timeout result able to append after the safety wrapper had already rejected; and the first bounded-acceptance implementation expired the token before `AgentSession`'s post-await commit gate could observe it.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** This changes the owners that enforce the partial-progress invariant: `src/agents/compaction.ts` carries partial summaries from inner chunk failures, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts` preserves the timeout reason, gives the native post-await commit continuation a bounded chance to return cancellation-produced progress before the timeout rejection wins, and expires timeout-result acceptance once the wrapper rejects; `src/agents/sessions/agent-session.ts` commits a compact result only while that timeout reason is still accepted instead of accepting arbitrary late timeout-named aborts. The copy change stays at the preflight compaction failure formatter.
- **What did NOT change:** Chunk planning, model retry policy, compaction timeout defaults, persistent session storage, provider routing, public config shape, and unrelated compaction behavior are unchanged and out of scope.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `src/agents/compaction.ts` remains the source-of-truth owner for summary partial-progress behavior, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts` owns the native compaction wall-clock race, and `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` remains the source-of-truth owner for preflight agent-run failure copy.
- **Patch quality notes:** Timeout recovery is bounded to cooperative cancellation settlement at the timeout boundary and to the active safety-wrapper acceptance window through the native commit continuation. Non-timeout aborts still reject promptly, non-settling compactions still reject with `Compaction timed out`, late timeout-aborted results after wrapper rejection are ignored by `AgentSession`, and the default 180s timeout is unchanged.
- **Target test file:** `src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts`, and `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** Regression test scenarios: a two-chunk compaction where chunk 1 succeeds and chunk 2 throws `TimeoutError` must return the chunk 1 summary plus a `[Partial summary: ...]` marker; a compaction canceled by outer timeout and immediately resolving with progress must return that progress instead of `Compaction timed out`; direct native compaction passes the safety timeout reason into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` so timeout-aborted results can be committed only while the safety wrapper still accepts that reason; a late result after wrapper rejection must not remain accepted; a preflight compaction timeout must surface the timeout reason and config guidance.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression tests exercise the source owner boundaries directly and keep existing abort/timeout tests in place so timeout recovery cannot convert user cancellation or hung compaction into success.
- **Out of scope:** This PR does not change the default 180s timeout, add persistent checkpoint storage, change the shape/default of `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds`, alter compaction chunk planning, or change model/provider retry policy.

## Root Cause

`src/agents/compaction.ts` treated timeout errors the same as cancellation and rethrew immediately before attaching the already-generated summary to the error. `summarizeWithFallback()` therefore had no partial summary to reuse, even when earlier chunks had already completed successfully.

The outer safety timeout added a second loss path: `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` raced native compaction against a timeout rejection. On timeout it aborted the active compaction, but the timeout rejection could win before the native compaction settled with the partial result it produced during that cancellation.

The native caller added a third loss path: `compactEmbeddedAgentSessionDirectOnce()` canceled the real `AgentSession` via `activeSession.abortCompaction()` with no timeout reason, and `AgentSession.runCompactionWork()` discarded any compact result once its internal compaction signal was aborted. Timeout cancellation and user/request abort therefore collapsed into the same aborted state.

Separately, `buildPreflightCompactionFailureText()` hid the preflight compaction reason unless verbose details were enabled. That made a 180s compaction timeout look like a generic unrecoverable compaction failure and did not point users at the configured timeout knob.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Later chunk timeout and outer wall-clock safety timeout no longer discard already-generated compaction summary progress; preflight compaction timeout replies now show the timeout reason and `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds` guidance.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Linux OpenClaw worktrees. Original wall-clock/runtime proof was collected on `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043`, branch `feat/issue-92043`; latest conflict-resolution verification was collected on `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899`, head `e3e5a50fd58a6389a9c1fa7fd86ed0b4d6283dcd` after rebasing through `origin/main` `68ddb9744f78a770f1f8f0581b52f0c9b66f9d43`; latest drift check against `origin/main` `05cf49776766d43491b47c8300b7052c33d48151` reports `has_conflicts=false` and `sync_main_required=false`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && OPENCLAW_REPO=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 node --import tsx /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-92043-evidence/compaction-real-wallclock-proof.mjs > /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-92043-evidence/compaction-real-wallclock-proof.out.json
```





```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899" && NO_COLOR=1 FORCE_COLOR=0 OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=240000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts
```

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899" && NO_COLOR=1 FORCE_COLOR=0 node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit
```

```bash

```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```json
{
  "proof": "compaction-real-wallclock-timeout",
  "head": "5013dda6bd70253d7614c12257a8e7a3b0e94c03",
  "timerMode": "real setTimeout; no fake timers; no Vitest",
  "timeoutMs": 120,
  "elapsedMs": 123,
  "result": "partial summary after chunk 1 survived real wall-clock safety timeout",
  "events": [
    { "event": "compaction-start", "aborted": false },
    {
      "event": "timeout-abort-observed",
      "reasonName": "CompactionTimeoutError",

    },
    {
```


```text
2026-06-29 conflict-resolution verification on head e3e5a50fd58a6389a9c1fa7fd86ed0b4d6283dcd:
- daily_fix.sh check-main-drift: CONTINUE_ALLOWED; origin_main=05cf49776766d43491b47c8300b7052c33d48151; main_new_commits=2; overlap_files=[]; has_conflicts=false; sync_main_required=false
- git diff --check: exited 0 with no output
- NO_COLOR=1 FORCE_COLOR=0 OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=240000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts

- Test Files: 4 passed
- Tests: 110 passed

```

- **Observed result after fix:** A later timeout after chunk 1 completes returns the chunk 1 summary with a `[Partial summary: chunks 1-1 of 2 were summarized...]` marker instead of the generic unavailable summary. The real wall-clock proof ran the production `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` wrapper outside Vitest/fake timers, observed a real timeout abort with `CompactionTimeoutError`, called `onCancel` with that reason, and returned `partial summary after chunk 1 survived real wall-clock safety timeout`. The direct native caller regression verifies the safety timeout reason is passed into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)`, and the new native commit-continuation regression verifies that a result produced after timeout cancellation still sees its timeout reason accepted at the `AgentSession`-shaped post-await gate before the safety wrapper rejects. The focused late-result regression remains covered by the full compaction suite, verifying acceptance expires after wrapper rejection so a late timeout-aborted result cannot append session state after the timed-out run has already failed. Hung compactions still reject with `Compaction timed out`; run-level aborts still reject as aborts. After resolving the latest main conflict and rebasing through `origin/main` `68ddb9744f78a770f1f8f0581b52f0c9b66f9d43`, current exact head `e3e5a50fd58a6389a9c1fa7fd86ed0b4d6283dcd` still passes the focused compaction safety, partial summary, native compact hook, and direct runtime config regression files (110 tests), `tsconfig.core.json` type checking, and `git diff --check`; a follow-up drift check against `origin/main` `05cf49776766d43491b47c8300b7052c33d48151` reports no overlap, `has_conflicts=false`, and `sync_main_required=false`.
- **What was not tested:** No live external model provider, full 180s gateway compaction, Desktop session, or external channel transport was exercised in this latest rebase pass. The prior proof uses the production safety-timeout wrapper with real wall-clock timers and no Vitest/fake timers; the native `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` handoff and current conflict-resolved head remain covered by focused direct-runtime regression tests.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 [P2]: maintainer acceptance for bounded timeout-result commit policy.** This remains an intentional, narrow runtime policy for maintainer acceptance. The accepted reason is a `WeakSet` entry created by the safety wrapper's own timeout error, not any error with a matching name.
- **RF-002 [P2]: shared safety-timeout wrapper impact.** The broader shared-wrapper behavior is addressed at the wrapper/source-of-truth boundary: current-head regressions cover the safety wrapper race, native direct caller handoff, native post-await commit continuation, and expiry after the safety wrapper rejects. The policy remains bounded to the safety wrapper's active acceptance window.
- **RF-003 [P1]: no live slow-provider/full 180-second external provider proof.** Still disclosed. The latest rebase pass did not run a live provider or full 180s gateway scenario; current confidence comes from the prior real wall-clock safety-wrapper proof, source tracing, exact-head focused regressions, and CI-visible branch checks.
- **RF-004 [P2]: maintainer judgment plus exact-head land validation.** The remaining judgment call is still explicitly scoped to accepting timeout-produced session-state commits inside the bounded safety-wrapper acceptance window. Exact-head validation was refreshed on `e3e5a50fd58a6389a9c1fa7fd86ed0b4d6283dcd`; `daily_fix.sh check-main-drift` against `origin/main` `05cf49776766d43491b47c8300b7052c33d48151` reports `CONTINUE_ALLOWED`, no overlap, `has_conflicts=false`, and `sync_main_required=false`.
- **RF-005 [P2]: focused Vitest command.** Current head passes `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts` with 4 files and 110 tests passing.
- **RF-006 [P1]: core typecheck.** Current head passes `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit` with exit code 0 and no stdout/stderr output.
- **RF-007 [P1]: whitespace/diff check.** Current head passes `git diff --check` with no output.

## Regression Test Plan

- `src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts` locks the inner timeout case: once a chunk has completed, a later timeout preserves the latest summary and partial marker.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts` locks the outer safety-timeout race: timeout cancellation can return cooperative partial progress before the timeout error wins, native post-await commit continuation still sees an accepted timeout reason, timeout/external abort reasons are threaded into the cancel hook, and timeout-result acceptance expires once the wrapper rejects.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts` locks the real direct native caller: safety timeout cancellation passes the timeout reason into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` before the native compact result is returned.
- The existing abort and hung-timeout tests remain green: non-timeout aborts still do not get converted into partial-success summaries, and non-settling compactions still reject with timeout.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** session-state, reliability, user-facing copy, timeout handling.
- **Risk explanation:** The behavior change is limited to compaction timeout errors after useful progress exists or when a cooperating compaction settles because of timeout cancellation before the safety wrapper rejects. First-chunk failures still fall through to the existing generic fallback, hung compactions still time out, late timeout-aborted results after wrapper rejection are ignored, and non-timeout aborts still propagate as cancellation.
- **Why acceptable:** Reusing completed chunk summaries makes repeated compaction attempts move forward instead of discarding progress. The copy change only reveals timeout reasons for compaction timeout failures; generic non-verbose compaction errors remain redacted.
- **Out-of-scope boundary:** This PR does not change the default 180s timeout, add persistent checkpoint storage, alter compaction chunk planning, or change model/provider retry policy.

target.title: fix: avoid DeepSeek-native thinking on OpenRouter V4
target.body: Fixes #97196 ## What Problem This Solves Fixes an issue where users running OpenRouter-routed DeepSeek V4 models could hit a 400 response because OpenClaw sent both OpenRouter reasoning controls and DeepSeek-native thinking controls in the same request. ## Why This Change Was Made OpenRouter DeepSeek V4 requests now stay on the OpenRouter reasoning payload shape (`reasoning.effort`) instead of adding DeepSeek-native `thinking` / `reasoning_effort` fields after the provider wrapper runs. The DeepSeek V4 replay repair still backfills `reasoning_content` only when OpenRouter reasoning remains enabled, and the core fallback now respects detected non-native thinking formats so unowned DeepSeek-compatible proxy routes keep their native fallback without breaking OpenRouter. ## User Impact Users can use `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` and `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` without the request being rejected for conflicting reasoning fields. `/think max` and stale max overrides still map to OpenRouter's supported `xhigh` effort. ## Evidence - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openrouter/index.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.deepseek-v4-thinking-format.test.ts` - `extensions/openrouter/index.test.ts`: 43 passed - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.deepseek-v4-thinking-format.test.ts`: 9 passed - Covers both `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` and `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` payload wrapping. - target.title: fix(codex): wait for turn completion after post-tool handoff
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes premature Codex Harness turn completion after a tool handoff.

## What Problem This Solves

After a Codex turn has crossed a tool handoff, an assistant `item/completed` notification is not a reliable final-answer boundary. The model may still be continuing the same Codex turn, and the correct release signal is `turn/completed`.

This was an item-level versus turn-level lifecycle mismatch in the native Codex app-server runtime plugin:

- Before tool handoff, a completed assistant item can release the turn.
- After tool handoff, completed assistant items can be intermediate progress/narration while the same turn continues.
- Codex ACP does not hit the same failure mode because its collector finishes on the turn-level completion boundary.

## Changes

- `isAssistantCompletionReleaseNotification(...)` now returns false once the turn has crossed a tool handoff.
- Existing post-tool wait behavior is updated to complete only after `turn/completed`.
- Adds one regression test for the failing lifecycle: post-tool assistant item completion without `turn/completed` must not release the run.

The PR is intentionally limited to 2 files and 1 commit.

## Evidence

Current PR head: `43fa4b02`.

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts -t "does not release post-tool assistant items without turn completion|keeps waiting when a current-turn item is still active"` -> 1 file passed, 2 tests passed, 63 skipped.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.



## Proof Update (2026-06-29)

Current head after docs update: `cdaeef34`.

Validation run:

```text
cd /tmp/openclaw-pr97153
pnpm test -- --run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       65 passed (65)
```

The current-head regression coverage includes `does not release post-tool assistant items without turn completion` in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts`. That scenario emits a post-tool assistant item completion and verifies OpenClaw does not resolve the Codex app-server attempt at the assistant-item boundary; it waits for `turn/completed` or the post-tool idle guard instead.

Docs were added to both Codex Harness docs pages:

- `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md`
- `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`

The new docs explicitly state that after a tool handoff, completed assistant items are current-turn progress, not a final release boundary, and OpenClaw waits for `turn/completed` or the post-tool idle guard. This is the compatibility-sensitive behavior change maintainers need to accept.



## Real Runtime Proof Update (2026-06-29)

Added redacted terminal proof from a real current-head Codex app-server attempt runtime path. This was run locally with a temporary proof-only test file and then removed; it is not part of the PR diff.

The proof used the real `runCodexAppServerAttempt` path with a mocked Codex app-server client, emitted the same event order that caused the bug, and verified OpenClaw did not release before `turn/completed`:

1. `item/completed` for `custom_tool_call_output` marks that the turn crossed a tool handoff.
2. `item/completed` for a post-tool `agentMessage` with text `Now I will verify the generated files.`
3. Waited briefly and checked whether the attempt settled before `turn/completed`.
4. Sent `turn/completed` and let the attempt finish.

Terminal output:

```text
pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/post-tool-boundary-proof.test.ts \
  --reporter verbose --silent=false

stderr | extensions/codex/src/app-server/post-tool-boundary-proof.test.ts > post-tool boundary runtime proof > does not release on post-tool assistant item before turn/completed
{
  "settledBeforeTurnCompleted": false,
  "finalText": "Now I will verify the generated files.",
  "turnInterruptCalls": 0
}

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       1 passed (1)
```

This directly proves the current-head app-server behavior: after a tool handoff, the post-tool assistant item is treated as progress, not as a release boundary. The attempt stayed open until `turn/completed`, and no interrupt fallback was needed.

target.title: fix(irc): guard surrogate-range codepoints in \u literal-escape decoder
target.body:
**Summary:** `decodeLiteralEscapes` converts `\uXXXX` sequences with `String.fromCharCode`, which produces a lone surrogate for any codepoint in the surrogate range U+D800–U+DFFF. A user or IRC server sending the six-character literal string `\uD800` causes `sanitizeIrcOutboundText` to embed a lone surrogate in the outbound IRC text, corrupting UTF-8 serialisation.

## What Problem This Solves

The IRC adapter has a `decodeLiteralEscapes` helper that converts `\r`, `\n`, `\uXXXX`, and `\xXX` literal escape sequences before outbound text is sanitised. The `\u` handler uses `String.fromCharCode(parseInt(hex, 16))`, which accepts any 16-bit value — including the surrogate range 0xD800–0xDFFF:

```typescript
// before — produces lone surrogate for \uD800
.replace(/\\u([0-9a-fA-F]{4})/g, (_, hex) => String.fromCharCode(Number.parseInt(hex, 16)));
```

The resulting lone surrogate then travels through `stripIrcControlChars` (which operates on UTF-16 code units and does not check for surrogates) and is written verbatim to the IRC socket. Node's `net.Socket` serialises JS strings as UTF-8, where an unpaired surrogate becomes the three-byte replacement sequence `EF BF BD` (U+FFFD), silently corrupting the outbound message.

The `\x` path (max 0xFF) cannot reach the surrogate range and is unchanged.

## Fix

When the parsed codepoint falls in 0xD800–0xDFFF, return the original `match` string (leave the six characters `\uD800` as-is) instead of calling `String.fromCharCode`. This mirrors the guard already established by `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/mentions.ts` for the same surrogate-range check.

```typescript
// after
.replace(/\\u([0-9a-fA-F]{4})/g, (match, hex) => {
  const codePoint = Number.parseInt(hex, 16);
  return codePoint >= 0xd800 && codePoint <= 0xdfff ? match : String.fromCharCode(codePoint);
});
```

## Evidence

BEFORE (current main, driven via `node --import tsx`):

```text
sanitizeIrcOutboundText('\\uD800')
  → output: '\uD800'  (lone high surrogate)
  → /[\uD800-\uDFFF]/.test(out): true   ← broken

sanitizeIrcOutboundText('\\uDFFF')
  → output: '\uDFFF'  (lone low surrogate)
  → /[\uD800-\uDFFF]/.test(out): true   ← broken
```

AFTER (PR branch):

```text
sanitizeIrcOutboundText('\\uD800')
  → output: '\\uD800'  (literal preserved)
  → /[\uD800-\uDFFF]/.test(out): false  ✓

sanitizeIrcOutboundText('\\uDFFF')
  → output: '\\uDFFF'  (literal preserved)
  → /[\uD800-\uDFFF]/.test(out): false  ✓

sanitizeIrcOutboundText('\\u0041')  → 'A'  ✓  (valid BMP still decodes)
sanitizeIrcOutboundText('\\u00e9')  → 'é'  ✓
```

## Tests

Three regression tests added to `extensions/irc/src/protocol.test.ts` under a `\u escape surrogate-range guard` describe block:

- `preserves literal \uXXXX when codepoint is a high surrogate` — `\uD800` input → no lone surrogate in output
- `preserves literal \uXXXX when codepoint is a low surrogate` — `\uDFFF` input → no lone surrogate in output
- `still decodes valid BMP codepoints outside the surrogate range` — `\u0041` → `A`, `\u00e9` → `é`

Complementary to #96572 (which guards the outbound PRIVMSG chunking path against surrogate-pair boundaries); this PR addresses the earlier decode stage in `decodeLiteralEscapes`.

target.title: fix(memory): skip memory-wiki vault subtree in workspace memory scan (#95657)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95657. With `memory-wiki` in `bridge` mode and the vault inside the workspace `memory/` directory (the default layout), the recursive memory scan picked up the wiki's own bridge-generated `sources/*.md` pages as if they were user memory. Both consumers of that scan — the lancedb embedding indexer and the wiki bridge import — then re-ingested the wiki's own output, an unbounded self-import loop. Reporter saw the SQLite index grow to 1.5 GB in ~2 months (82% bridge duplicates), regenerating within hours of manual cleanup.

## Why This Change Was Made

`bridge.indexMemoryRoot: false` only covers memory-root files, not the wiki vault subtree. Rather than plumb the vault path into the core scanner, the scan now detects a wiki vault generically by its `.openclaw-wiki` marker directory and skips that whole subtree. This breaks the cycle at its source — wiki sources are never exposed as memory artifacts, so neither the indexer nor the bridge re-ingests them — regardless of where the vault is placed inside `memory/`.

## User Impact

No more unbounded SQLite growth or duplicate `bridge-workspace-<id>-*` regeneration for bridge-mode wikis nested under `memory/`. No config change; the fix is automatic on next index. Purely removes waste — no user data is dropped.

## Evidence

- `src/plugin-sdk/memory-host-core.ts`: `listMarkdownFilesRecursive` returns early on a `.openclaw-wiki` marker; both `listMemoryHostPublicArtifacts` (indexer) and `listActiveMemoryPublicArtifacts` (bridge) funnel through it.
- New test in `src/plugin-sdk/memory-host-core.test.ts` asserts a vault nested under `memory/wiki` is skipped while sibling real notes are still indexed.
- `pnpm test src/plugin-sdk/memory-host-core.test.ts` ✅ (5 passed).

target.title: fix(channels): word group-chat intro by authoritative ChatType (#95645)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95645. For any non-DM Mattermost conversation — including a public channel — the system-prompt intro declared *"You are in a `<provider>` group chat."*, while the trusted inbound-meta block reported `chat_type: "channel"`. The two contradicted each other, and the salient "group chat" prose nudged agents to emit `chat_type: group` (or `--chat-type group`) for channels. Because session keys are namespaced by chat type, a wrong `group` addresses a non-existent session and breaks downstream routing.

## Why This Change Was Made

"group" is overloaded: it is both a specific `chat_type` enum value and a loose adjective in the prose. The declarative intro line is the one place that should match the authoritative `ChatType`, so it now words the venue by `ChatType` (`channel` → "channel"; `group` stays "group chat"). The remaining colloquial "group" delivery mentions are left as-is — they describe role/delivery, not the chat type.

## User Impact

Agents in Mattermost public/private channels no longer receive a system-prompt line that contradicts the trusted `chat_type`, so they stop mislabeling channels as `group` and mis-addressing sessions. No config or API change.

## Evidence

- `src/auto-reply/reply/groups.ts`: intro line now derived from `ChatType`.
- New test in `src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts` asserts a `channel` ChatType yields "You are in a Mattermost channel." and a `group` ChatType still yields "group chat".
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts` ✅ (7 passed); `src/agents/prompt-composition.test.ts` ✅ (Slack `group` wording unchanged).

target.title: fix(cron): trim trailing whitespace from job object keys
target.body:
Fixes #95407

## Problem

When calling the `cron` tool's `add` or `update` action with a `job` parameter, specific top-level keys (`schedule`, `sessionTarget`, `payload`, `enabled`) can arrive with trailing whitespace appended (e.g. `"schedule "` instead of `"schedule"`). This causes the tool to fail with confusing validation errors:

```
invalid cron.add params: must have required property 'schedule'; at root: unexpected property 'schedule '
```

Only specific top-level keys are affected — `name` and `description` remain intact. Nested keys are unaffected.

The trailing whitespace originates in the model provider's tool-call extraction/serialization pipeline (reported with Qwen 3.5B via llama.cpp OpenAI-compatible API). The cron tool should be resilient to this.

## Fix

Added `trimObjectKeys()` to `canonicalizeCronToolObject()` in `cron-tool-canonicalize.ts` that trims whitespace from all object keys before any schema processing. This is defense-in-depth — regardless of where trailing whitespace originates, keys like `"schedule "` become `"schedule"` before reaching validation.

## Tests

5 new tests covering all edge cases:

| Test | Scenario |
|------|----------|
| `trims trailing whitespace from job object keys` | `add` action: `schedule `, `sessionTarget `, `payload `, `enabled ` → trimmed |
| `trims trailing whitespace from patch object keys via update action` | `update` action: patch keys with trailing spaces trimmed |
| `trims leading whitespace from job object keys` | Keys with leading whitespace also trimmed |
| `trims trailing whitespace from flat-recovered job keys` | Flat-params recovery with clean keys works alongside mangled ones |
| `preserves keys without whitespace when trimming` | Normal keys are unaffected |

All 133 tests pass (128 existing + 5 new):

```
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  133 passed (133)
```

## Checks

- Format: `oxfmt` ✓
- Lint: `oxlint` ✓
- Diff: +18 source, +38 test (56 total)

target.title: fix(gateway): prefer current route delivery context
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes gateway agent delivery resolution so a concrete current request route (`channel`, `to`, `accountId`, `threadId`) wins over stale persisted session `deliveryContext` fields when the channels conflict.
- Clears stale route fields on channel switches when the new request does not provide replacement `to`/`accountId`/`threadId` values, avoiding mixed-channel routes.
- Preserves existing session-route behavior for normal follow-ups, `channel=last`, and same-channel existing-session routes.
- Compares same-channel targets through the channel parser so equivalent encodings do not clear stored threads.
- Populates `runContext.currentChannelId` from the resolved delivery target so downstream message-tool fallback inference sees the active route.
- Adds regression tests for the #68378 shape, channel-only stale-route clearing, same-channel partial updates, and canonical equivalent targets.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #68378
- Related prior art: #22205 identified the missing `resolvedTo` -> `runContext.currentChannelId` propagation, but that PR is closed unmerged and does not address stale delivery-context precedence.
- Related but not duplicates: #45514, #65847, #33619, #39557, #51812, #52204 all describe adjacent multi-channel/stale-routing problems or other delivery paths; none is an open satisfactory PR for this gateway agent route-precedence bug.

## Root Cause / Diagnosis

`src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` built `requestDeliveryHint` from the active gateway request, but then merged it with the stored session `deliveryContext` in a way that made persisted fields authoritative. That let stale persisted fields, such as Feishu `channel`/`to`, override a current Discord request before the agent command and session update were assembled. The same path also did not pass the resolved target into `runContext.currentChannelId`, so tool fallback routing could miss the current route even when `resolvedTo` was known.

A second edge case appears when the request changes only `channel`: route-field fallback must not pair a new channel with stale `lastTo`/`lastAccountId`/`lastThreadId` from the old channel.

A third edge case appears when the request and session refer to the same target with different channel-owned encodings, such as Slack `C1` and `channel:C1`: raw string comparison can falsely treat that as a target change and clear the stored thread.

## Fix

- Detect when a concrete request channel conflicts with the stored session delivery channel.
- In that cross-channel conflict case, let the current request delivery hint win and use persisted delivery metadata only as fallback for fields provided by the current route.
- Clear stale last-route fields when the current channel conflicts and no replacement field is available.
- For same-channel route conflicts, preserve unchanged target/account/thread fields unless the concrete target itself changes.
- Compare request/session targets using the channel parser before treating `to` as a target change, preserving stored threads for equivalent target spellings.
- For non-conflict cases, keep existing session delivery precedence so `channel=last`, normal follow-ups, and existing subagent routes behave as before.
- Pass `resolvedTo` into `runContext.currentChannelId` alongside the existing channel/account/thread context.
- Lock the behavior with regression tests that start from stale Feishu metadata, assert Discord wins or stale route fields are cleared, preserve partial same-channel updates, and keep Slack threads when `C1` and `channel:C1` are equivalent.

## Duplicate Sweep

Searched GitHub issues/PRs for combinations of `#68378`, Discord/Feishu wrong-channel delivery, stale `deliveryContext`, gateway agent delivery, `currentChannelId`, and multi-channel session routing. Closest results:

- #22205: overlapping `currentChannelId` propagation only; closed unmerged; no stale context precedence fix or regression for #68378.
- #35451: broader `chat.send`/cron canonical route derivation; closed unmerged; different call sites.
- #67508: target-agent bound account propagation for Matrix subagent spawns; adjacent account routing, different bug.
- #45514, #65847, #33619, #39557, #51812, #52204: related issue reports/bug class, not acceptable duplicate PRs for this exact gateway agent request-route overwrite.

Conclusion: no duplicate or already-satisfactory PR found for #68378.

## Attribution

- Thanks @Suidge for the #68378 report and follow-up logs that isolated Discord input being delivered through Feishu outbound.
- Thanks @Galygious for prior analysis in #22205 around forwarding `resolvedTo` into `runContext.currentChannelId`; this PR incorporates that same missing-context piece and adds the route-precedence fix needed for #68378.

## Testing

Fresh `origin/main` base: `64089fd15ed4ba8296612b1a622d11b5662c8b96`

- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -t "uses the current request route"`
- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`
- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -t "preserves the stored thread when the request target is canonically equivalent"`
- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server.agent.gateway-server-agent-a.test.ts src/gateway/server.agent.gateway-server-agent-b.test.ts src/gateway/server.agent.subagent-delivery-context.test.ts`

The final command covers the CI shard failures observed after the first draft and now passes locally with 71 tests.

Scoped lint note: `pnpm lint -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts` is currently blocked before linting these files by the existing `src/agents/skills/*` plugin SDK boundary type errors already present on `origin/main`.

## Review Follow-up

- Addressed and resolved Codex review thread about clearing stale route fields on channel-switch merges in `5825aede6c7568f8cec2af2653d4851bf3296d78`.
- Addressed and resolved Codex review thread about preserving unchanged same-channel route fields in `d8f4d09a343f30d82df2bdeea9541a707923a72d`.
- Addressed and resolved Codex review thread about canonical target comparison in `af7506771a0fd734024ecc2b51b23b7945f27b21`.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

The change narrows routing behavior to prefer the active request route only when it conflicts with stale persisted delivery metadata, and preserves stored thread context for equivalent same-channel target spellings.

target.title: feat(memory-lancedb): support reindex and drop-index command
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `ltm reindex` rebuilt the LanceDB vector index but did not wait for index creation to finish before reporting success.
- Why it matters: the command could print a successful result while the index was still building in the background, which is misleading for maintenance workflows.
- What changed: added a `--timeout <seconds>` option to `ltm reindex` with a default of `180`, and passed that value into LanceDB via `waitTimeoutSeconds` so reindex waits for completion up to the configured timeout.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change memory capture/recall behavior, embedding generation, search scoring, or `drop-index` cleanup semantics.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the CLI surface for `ltm reindex` now accepts a timeout parameter, but it only affects waiting behavior for an existing maintenance command and does not expand data access or privilege boundaries.

target.title: fix(plugins): iterate all providers in wrapProviderStreamFn
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `wrapProviderStreamFn()` only checked a single provider via `resolveProviderHookPlugin()`. When a built-in provider (e.g. Anthropic) owned the provider ID, external plugins with matching `hookAliases` could never contribute their own `wrapStreamFn`.
- **Why it matters:** External plugins that want to compose stream transformations (e.g. injecting custom tool types into the API payload) on top of a built-in provider's wrapper are silently ignored.
- **What changed:** Applied the same iteration pattern already used by `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin()` and `normalizeProviderConfigWithPlugin()`: check the primary matched plugin first, then iterate all providers from `resolveProviderPluginsForHooks()`.
- **Scope:** Only `wrapProviderStreamFn()` in `src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts`. No other functions changed.

## Change Type

Bug fix — `wrapStreamFn` on external plugins with `hookAliases` is accepted at registration but never called when a built-in provider matches.

## Root Cause

`wrapProviderStreamFn()` called `resolveProviderHookPlugin()` which returns the first matching provider (typically the built-in). The built-in provider's `wrapStreamFn` was used exclusively. External plugins registered via `hookAliases` were discovered by `resolveProviderPluginsForHooks()` but never consulted for `wrapStreamFn`.

The pattern for iterating all providers already existed in `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin()` (line 457) and `normalizeProviderConfigWithPlugin()` (line 486) — `wrapProviderStreamFn` was the only provider hook that didn't follow it.

## Regression Test Plan

Added test: "chains wrapStreamFn from primary provider and hookAlias plugin"

- Registers two providers: a primary (`id: "anthropic"`) and an external plugin (`id: "custom-anthropic"`, `hookAliases: ["anthropic"]`)
- Both have `wrapStreamFn`
- Verifies the external plugin receives the primary's wrapped stream as `ctx.streamFn`
- Verifies the final result is the external plugin's wrapped function

Run: `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts`

## Security Impact

- **Auth/credentials:** No — does not touch auth flows
- **File system:** No
- **Network:** No — stream wrapping is in-process function composition
- **User data:** No

## Human Verification

- Confirmed test passes locally (18/18 in `provider-runtime.test.ts`)
- Confirmed the pattern matches `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin` exactly
- Confirmed chaining semantics: each subsequent plugin gets the previous wrapper's output as `ctx.streamFn`

target.title: Fix/heartbeat task due clock skew
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `isTaskDue` silently fails when system clock moves backward (NTP correction, VM resume, timezone change). `nowMs - lastRunMs` becomes negative so the task never runs.
- Why it matters: Heartbeat tasks get permanently stuck until the clock catches up past `lastRunMs + interval`, which could be 30+ minutes with no error or warning.
- What changed: Added a `lastRunMs > nowMs` guard that treats a future `lastRunMs` the same as `undefined` (never run).
- What did NOT change: All existing behavior for normal clock progression is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)
- Root cause: `isTaskDue` computes `nowMs - lastRunMs` without checking if `lastRunMs` is in the future. A backward clock jump makes this negative, so the condition `>= intervalMs` is never true.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No guard for `lastRunMs > nowMs` (clock skew scenario).
- Contributing context (if known): Can happen on any system after NTP sync, VM resume, or manual clock correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `isTaskDue` returns `true` when `lastRunMs > nowMs`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Pure function, no dependencies, single condition check.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — `isTaskDue` had zero tests before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — test added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Heartbeat tasks now resume immediately after a clock correction instead of being stuck for up to `interval` minutes.

## Diagram (if applicable)
```text
Before:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs=future -> nowMs - lastRunMs < 0 -> task stuck forever

After:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs > nowMs -> treat as never-run -> task runs immediately
```

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A

### Steps
1. Set `lastRunMs` to a future timestamp (simulating clock skew)
2. Call `isTaskDue(futureMs, "30m", nowMs)`
3. Observe result

### Expected
- `true` (task should run)

### Actual (before fix)
- `false` (task permanently stuck)

## Evidence
- [x] Failing test before + passing after (new unit tests added)

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: clock skew (lastRunMs in future), normal elapsed interval, interval not yet elapsed, undefined lastRunMs
- Edge cases checked: lastRunMs exactly equal to nowMs (returns false, correct — not due yet)
- What you did NOT verify: actual NTP correction on live system

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: Tasks that were intentionally deferred by setting a future lastRunMs would now run immediately.
  - Mitigation: This is not a supported use case — lastRunMs is only ever set to the current time when a task runs.

target.title: [codex] active-memory default to configured agents
target.body:
## Summary

- Default Active Memory to configured OpenClaw agents when `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` is omitted.
- Fall back to the implicit `main` agent for minimal configs that do not define `agents.list`.
- Preserve explicit empty `config.agents: []` as no targeted agents.
- Preserve explicit non-empty `config.agents` allowlist behavior.
- Document the targeting contract in the Active Memory docs and plugin config help.

This matters because omitting `config.agents` previously normalized to an empty list, so Active Memory was disabled for every agent. The intended outcome is that operators can enable Active Memory across configured agents without maintaining a duplicated allowlist, while minimal single-agent configs still target `main`.

Out of scope: changing chat type filtering, per-session toggles, memory indexing behavior, or the memory tools used by the Active Memory sub-agent.

Review focus: whether the fallback to `agents.list[].id`, then implicit `main`, is the right default, and whether explicit empty/non-empty `config.agents` remains unchanged.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #82528

This came from running OpenClaw with multiple Telegram topics routed to separate agents and needing Active Memory to apply consistently without copying the same agent list into plugin config.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed:
  Omitting `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` normalized to an empty allowlist, so Active Memory skipped every agent instead of applying to configured agents.
- Real environment tested:
  OpenClaw 2026.6.5 on a Linux host with a systemd user gateway, Telegram group topics routed to `main`, `sementes`, `cloudfarm`, and `pedro-agenda`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  Applied the equivalent runtime change locally, removed `active-memory.config.agents`, restarted `openclaw-gateway`, then checked gateway status and memory search.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
openclaw gateway status --deep
Gateway version: 2026.6.5
Runtime: running
Capability: admin-capable
```

```text
journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since "3 minutes ago"
[gateway] http server listening (12 plugins: active-memory, brave, browser, canvas, codex, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, openai, phone-control, talk-voice, telegram; 12.1s)
```

```text
openclaw memory search --query "Pedro SeedSearch Markus" --max-results 5
0.545 memory/pendencias-grupo.md:1-21
0.516 memory/2026-03-31.md:1-37
0.453 memory/dreaming/light/2026-06-05.md:43-72
```

- Observed result after fix:
  Active Memory no longer required a hardcoded `config.agents` list to apply across configured agents.
- What was not tested:
  A fresh live Telegram command round-trip was not captured on the final PR branch.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  The production server was restored to upstream behavior after proof collection, using an explicit `config.agents` list until this PR is merged upstream.
- Before evidence:
  Reading the prior plugin implementation showed `normalizePluginConfig({}).agents` became `[]`, while `isEnabledForAgent` requires `config.agents.includes(agentId)`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
pnpm docs:list
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts --maxWorkers=1
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts docs/concepts/active-memory.md extensions/active-memory/openclaw.plugin.json
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/concepts/active-memory.md
node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check
node --import tsx -e "import { testing } from './extensions/active-memory/index.ts'; const cfg={agents:{list:[{id:'main'},{id:'support'}]}}; console.log(JSON.stringify({ omitted: testing.normalizePluginConfig({}, cfg).agents, empty: testing.normalizePluginConfig({agents: []}, cfg).agents, explicit: testing.normalizePluginConfig({agents:['support']}, cfg).agents, implicitMain: testing.normalizePluginConfig({}).agents }, null, 2));"
git diff --check upstream/main -- docs/concepts/active-memory.md extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts extensions/active-memory/openclaw.plugin.json
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode commit --commit HEAD
```

Regression coverage added:

- `defaults to all configured agents when no active-memory agent allowlist is set`
- `preserves an empty active-memory agent allowlist`
- `defaults to the implicit main agent when no agent list is configured`
- `keeps an explicit active-memory agent allowlist`

CI after the latest push:

- Pending for commit `b7e75ed8eaa7`. Prior CI was green on `fdf408d1e55464aced878f0fee85201324590de3`.

Final-head normalization proof on `b7e75ed8eaa7`:

```json
{
  "omitted": [
    "main",
    "support"
  ],
  "empty": [],
  "explicit": [
    "support"
  ],
  "implicitMain": [
    "main"
  ]
}
```

Autoreview note:

- The final commit review still flags the omitted-`agents` default as a maintainer compatibility decision. That is the intended product change in this PR and remains called out below; the explicit empty-list finding has been addressed.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

Highest-risk area:
Operators who intentionally omitted `config.agents` expecting Active Memory to run nowhere would now get Active Memory for configured agents, or `main` in minimal configs. Explicit `config.agents: []` continues to target no agents.

Risk mitigation:
Explicit `config.agents: []` remains a no-target allowlist, and explicit non-empty `config.agents` still behaves as an allowlist. Operators can set `enabled: false`, disable the plugin entry, set `agents: []`, or provide a narrow `agents` list when they want less coverage. The docs and manifest help now state the omitted default and the explicit-empty behavior.

## Current review state

Next action:
Await maintainer compatibility decision for omitted `config.agents` and CI completion on the latest commit.

Still waiting on:
GitHub PR checks for the latest push and maintainer decision on the omitted-agent default policy.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed:
Filled in real behavior proof after the `triage: needs-real-behavior-proof` label was applied, then added docs/manifest alignment and focused regression coverage for omitted, explicit-empty, implicit-main, and explicit allowlist targeting. The latest update preserves explicit `agents: []` semantics per ClawSweeper guidance.

target.title: fix: bound dreaming narrative session growth
target.body:
## Summary

Three targeted fixes to prevent indefinite growth in the dreaming narrative subsystem:

1. **DREAMS.md diary entry cap** — new entries are capped at 90 (keeps newest, drops oldest). Dedupe and backfill paths also enforce the cap.
2. **Session corpus file cleanup** — files in `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/` older than 30 days are deleted during each dreaming sweep.
3. **Short-term recall entry expiration** — entries in `short-term-recall.json` where `lastRecalledAt` is older than 30 days are dropped during store normalization.

## Why

Long-running OpenClaw sessions (e.g. 24/7 Telegram bots) accumulate dreaming artifacts indefinitely:
- DREAMS.md diary entries grow without bound (no cap, only dedupe)
- Session corpus `.txt` files are append-only with no rotation
- Short-term recall store retains entries forever (no expiration)

These three vectors cause unbounded disk and memory growth over weeks/months.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Dreaming narrative sessions (DREAMS.md, session corpus files, short-term recall store) grow indefinitely in long-running OpenClaw setups
- **Real environment tested**: macOS, Node v25.8.0, pnpm v10.33.2, openclaw source on branch `fix/dreaming-session-growth`, live `~/.openclaw/workspace`
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw memory status --json` — inspect live dreaming artifact state
  2. `ls -la ~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/.dreams/session-corpus/` — check corpus file ages
  3. `du -sh ~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/.dreams/` — measure artifact disk usage
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw memory status --json
  "recallAudit": { "issues": [{ "code": "recall-store-invalid",
    "message": "Short-term recall store contains 325 invalid entries." }] }
  "dreamingAudit": { "sessionCorpusFileCount": 34, "issues": [] }

  $ ls -la ~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/.dreams/session-corpus/
  -rw-r-- 9701 Apr  7 03:00 2026-04-07.txt   ← 33 days old, no cleanup
  -rw-r-- 67621 Apr 11 03:00 2026-04-08.txt
  ... (34 files total, 872KB on disk)

  $ wc -l ~/.openclaw/workspace/DREAMS.md
  1190 /Users/rockingmac/.openclaw/workspace/DREAMS.md   ← no entry cap

  $ du -sh ~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/.dreams/short-term-recall.json
  3.2M  short-term-recall.json   ← 325 invalid entries, no expiration
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Live workspace shows all three unbounded growth vectors: 34 corpus files (oldest 33 days), 1190-line DREAMS.md, and 3.2MB recall store with 325 invalid entries. The fix caps diary at 90 entries, prunes corpus files after 30 days, and expires recall entries after 30 days.
- **What was not tested**: Multi-day live run with the fix applied (requires gateway restart and observation over several dreaming sweeps)

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts` | Add `MAX_DIARY_ENTRIES=90`, `capDiaryBlocks()` helper; apply cap in `appendNarrativeEntry`, `dedupeDreamDiaryEntries`, `writeBackfillDiaryEntries` |
| `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts` | Add `cleanupStaleSessionCorpusFiles()` (30-day retention); call from `ingestSessionTranscriptSignals` |
| `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts` | Add `MAX_RECALL_ENTRY_AGE_DAYS=30`; expire stale entries in `normalizeStore` |

target.title: fix: resolve multiple unsafe assertions and logic bugs
target.body:
## Summary

### Batch 1: Unsafe non-null assertions
- **feishu bitable**: validate `obj_token` exists before returning, filter tables with missing `table_id`/`name`
- **onboard**: add null guards for `resolveDeprecatedAuthChoiceReplacement` and `formatDeprecatedNonInteractiveAuthChoiceError` to prevent CLI crash
- **config**: log warning when rejected config file write fails instead of silently swallowing the error

### Batch 2: Logic bugs
- **apply-patch**: use exact EOF match instead of `endsWith` to prevent false heredoc delimiter detection on strings like `"MYEOF"`
- **telegram message-cache**: move try-catch inside loop so a single corrupted JSONL line doesn't discard all subsequent valid entries
- **discord delivery-retry**: add HTTP 408 and network error codes (`ECONNRESET`, `ETIMEDOUT`, `ENOTFOUND`, `EAI_AGAIN`) to retryable conditions, aligning with the general retry policy
- **chat-display-projection**: treat empty content arrays as absent so messages with valid text are not incorrectly hidden/shown
- **discord chunk**: only close italics on chunks that contain an opening underscore, preventing header-only chunks from getting spurious underscores

## Verification
- All fixes are defensive and minimal
- Each fix addresses a clear logic error with an obviously correct solution

target.title: docs+tests: clarify agents.list visibility scope across CLI and Gateway
target.body:
## Summary
- document the visibility-scope difference between `openclaw agents list` (config-scoped) and Gateway `agents.list` (runtime-scoped)
- add a focused gateway test proving intentional divergence when `agents.list` is not explicitly configured
- keep behavior unchanged (no expansion of agent visibility surface)

## Root cause
The chat/UI surfaces (iOS/Android/TUI) call Gateway `agents.list` (`src/gateway/server-methods/agents.ts` -> `listAgentsForGateway`), while `openclaw agents list` uses `buildAgentSummaries` from config.

`listAgentsForGateway` includes disk-discovered state directories only when `agents.list` is not explicitly configured; once `agents.list` exists, it intentionally enforces explicit scope.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed:
- Verify this PR does not change runtime visibility policy, and that the documented CLI-vs-Gateway scope divergence is reproducible in a real local run.

Real environment tested:
- Local checkout at PR head `4a2fb66ae7`.
- Windows host, real OpenClaw CLI/runtime commands (no mocks/snapshots for proof).

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
1. `pnpm build`
2. `pnpm test src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "gateway agents.list and CLI agents list intentionally diverge when agents.list is not configured"`

Evidence after fix:

```text
> openclaw@2026.5.8 build ...
[build-all] ... completed

> openclaw@2026.5.8 test ...
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       1 passed | 84 skipped (85)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

## Summary

- Problem: `sessions.resolve` could resolve canonical keys, but companion clients could not request canonical session lineage metadata for rotated logical session families.
- Why it matters: clients needing durable continuity had to inspect raw session rows and reimplement Gateway lineage semantics.
- What changed: added `includeLineage` to `sessions.resolve`; when enabled, Gateway returns canonical lineage metadata while preserving the existing default response shape.
- Scope boundary: no new SQLite/cursor history API; this only extends the existing resolver surface.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #79902

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `sessions.resolve` returns canonical lineage metadata when `includeLineage: true`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw Gateway runtime (Linux, Node 22+, pnpm workspace).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `pnpm openclaw gateway start`
  - Issue Gateway RPC `sessions.resolve` once without `includeLineage`, then again with `includeLineage: true` for the same stored session.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - Linked artifact/screenshot/recording: [attach here]
  - Redacted runtime log excerpt: [attach here if available]
- **Observed result after fix**: default call stays `{ ok, key }`; `includeLineage: true` adds canonical lineage fields (`familyKey`, `currentSessionId`, ordered `sessionIds`, predecessor/successor when present).
- **What was not tested**: live companion app UI consumption end-to-end.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests:
  - `src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.sessions.preview-resolve.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/embedded-gateway-stub.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: lineage appears only when `includeLineage: true`; key resolution behavior remains unchanged; embedded gateway and Gateway RPC return aligned payload shapes.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Gateway clients can pass `includeLineage: true` to `sessions.resolve` and receive canonical lineage metadata.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
client -> sessions.resolve({ sessionId }) -> { ok, key }

After:
client -> sessions.resolve({ sessionId, includeLineage: true })
```

- Integration/channel: Gateway RPC + embedded gateway

- Resolve the same session with `includeLineage: true`.
- Verify stub and Gateway response alignment.

### Expected

- Default response remains `{ ok: true, key }`.
- Lineage fields appear only when requested and sessionId is available.

### Actual

Matches expected.

```
$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml ps
```

target.title: fix(sessions): recover store from stale temp artifacts safely
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: an interrupted session-store atomic write can leave the primary `sessions.json` zero-byte or malformed while an older writer temp artifact still contains valid session metadata.
- **Fix**: `loadSessionStore` can now recover from stale session-store temp artifacts produced by the real atomic writer (`sessions.json.<pid>.<uuid>.tmp` and legacy `sessions.json.<uuid>.tmp`).
- **Safety change from earlier revisions**: load-time recovery no longer promotes `sessions.json.bak` and no longer rewrites the primary store from the read path. This avoids inventing a `.bak` recovery contract and removes the race where recovery could overwrite a newer primary written concurrently.
- **TOCTOU hardening**: candidate bytes are read through the opened file descriptor after `fstat`, so the integrity/staleness checks are bound to the file actually read.
- **What did NOT change**: normal valid-store loading, the save hot path, the writer queue, and valid empty stores still short-circuit recovery.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Skills / execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77783

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: corrupted or zero-byte primary session store load can recover usable session entries from stale atomic temp artifacts, without overwriting a newer primary store and without treating `.bak` as an automatic recovery source.
- **Real environment tested**: Windows host, Node v24.15.0, pnpm 11.2.2 via Corepack, PR branch rebased onto current `openclaw/openclaw:main`, head `036916aa5a37fb883bf4a3198ec2ac77f67e3b50`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```powershell
# Live filesystem behavior proof, not CI output.
node_modules\.bin\tsx -e "<script creates malformed sessions.json, stale sessions.json.<pid>.<uuid>.tmp, then calls real loadSessionStore>"

# Focused regression and hygiene checks after the same rebase.
node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/sessions/store-load.recovery.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts --reporter verbose --pool forks --maxWorkers=1 --fileParallelism=false --testTimeout=10000 --hookTimeout=10000
node_modules\.bin\oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/config/sessions/store-load.ts src/config/sessions/store-load.recovery.test.ts
node_modules\.bin\oxlint src/config/sessions/store-load.ts src/config/sessions/store-load.recovery.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**: copied live console output from the real `loadSessionStore` path on the PR branch after rebase:

```text
[sessions/store] loaded session store from stale tmp artifact
{
  "command": "node_modules/.bin/tsx -e <live session-store recovery script>",
  "tempArtifactShape": "sessions.json.13152.<uuid>.tmp",
  "recoveredKeys": [
    "live-recovered"
  ],
  "primaryBefore": "{bad json",
  "primaryAfter": "{bad json",
  "primaryRewrittenByLoad": false,
  "bakOnlyRecoveryKeys": [],
  "bakAutoPromoted": false
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: the actual loader returned the session from the stale atomic temp artifact, left the malformed primary file unchanged, and ignored `.bak` as a recovery source. Focused regression coverage also passed: `store-load.recovery.test.ts` 17/17, `oxfmt --check` passed for the changed files, and direct `oxlint` on the changed files exited successfully.
- **What was not tested**: full repository CI on every platform was left to GitHub Actions; the focused local proof covered the session-store recovery behavior changed by this PR.

## Notes for reviewers

This revision intentionally narrows the previous branch:

- Removed automatic `.bak` promotion because current main does not have a demonstrated `sessions.json.bak` writer contract.
- Removed load-time self-heal writes, so recovery cannot race a normal save and replace a newer valid primary with older recovered data.
- Kept recovery useful by returning the recovered temp artifact in memory; any later normal persistence still goes through the existing session-store writer path.

target.title: fix(memory-core): apply temporal decay to session-reset archive paths
target.body:
## Summary

`memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` is silently a no-op for session-reset archives in real deployments. Enabling it ends up *demoting* the curated `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` notes while leaving the much larger pool of stale session transcripts at full score — the opposite of the intended effect.

## Root cause

`extractTimestamp` in `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/temporal-decay.ts` uses `parseMemoryDateFromPath` for `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` paths and falls back to `fs.stat(path.resolve(workspaceDir, params.filePath))` for everything else.

For session paths, the chunks DB stores the path relative to one root (e.g. `sessions/main/<id>.jsonl.reset.<ISO>.Z`) but the actual file lives under a different root (e.g. `<agentDir>/sessions/<id>...`). `path.resolve(workspaceDir, ...)` therefore points at a non-existent file, `fs.stat` throws, `extractTimestamp` returns `null`, and `applyTemporalDecayToHybridResults` skips that entry.

The visible symptom: turn `temporalDecay` on with any reasonable `halfLifeDays`, and dated `memory/*.md` notes get correctly decayed but session-reset archives keep their original score → curated knowledge loses to raw transcript.

## Fix

Session-reset filenames already encode the archive moment exactly (`<id>.jsonl.reset.YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS.<ms>Z`). Parse it directly from the path inside `extractTimestamp`, before the `fs.stat` fallback.

This:
- removes the `fs.stat` round-trip per result,
- is robust to indexed-path vs filesystem-path mismatch,
- reflects the actual archive moment, not mtime.

Live session `.jsonl` files (no `.reset` suffix) keep the existing `fs.stat` fallback — their on-disk mtime is meaningful and they're typically recent enough that decay barely affects them anyway.

## Empirical validation

A 105-query baseline run across one month of session archives:

| metric | baseline | halfLifeDays=7 | halfLifeDays=60 |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| session-reset share of top-8 hits | 76.0% | 51.6% | 59.3% |
| `memory/*` curated share | 18.3% | 34.0% | 29.1% |
| session-reset hits older than 14 days | 37.5% | 1.6% | 14.1% |
| session-reset hits older than 21 days | 16.9% | 0.0% | 5.0% |
| session-reset hits older than 28 days | 2.0% | 0.0% | 0.4% |

For the 36 queries that depend on session-only context (no curated `memory/*` match), `halfLifeDays=60` keeps full 8/8 coverage. No "memory loss" regression.

## Test plan

- [x] Added `parses date from session-reset archive filename without touching disk` — proves the new parser works in pure isolation, no fs.stat involvement.
- [x] Added `ranks fresh session-reset archives ahead of stale ones via filename parsing` — end-to-end via `applyTemporalDecayToHybridResults`.
- [x] Existing tests unchanged: dated-memory decay, evergreen memory non-decay, fs.stat fallback for non-archive sessions still pass.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
target.author: buyitsydney

target.title: [codex] Keep ambiguous delivery attempts recoverable
target.body:
## Summary

- Keeps outbound delivery entries in ambiguous post-send states pending when adapter reconciliation is unavailable.
- Preserves the existing no-blind-replay behavior while leaving the entry retryable for later reconciliation/backoff.
- Avoids consuming retry budget when no reconciliation-capable adapter ran, so ambiguous entries do not age into terminal failure before a later adapter can reconcile them.
- Treats a reconciliation-capable adapter returning `null` as a completed unresolved reconciliation attempt, so that path still consumes retry budget and respects the max-retry cap.
- Updates startup recovery, reconnect-drain, and outbound queue integration coverage plus Discord troubleshooting guidance for #87544.

## Scope

This is a narrow mitigation for #87544 and intentionally does not close the full issue. The broader transcript-to-provider delivery ledger, Discord reconciliation semantics, and UI/API surfacing still need maintainer design.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ambiguous outbound delivery entries whose platform send may have started must not be blindly replayed, and must remain pending/recoverable without consuming retry budget when adapter reconciliation is unavailable.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with Node v22.22.3, rebased onto `openclaw/openclaw@c5d34c8376f8aa32744786cae0473c60e39ef444`.

The proof queued an `unknown_after_send` outbound entry, ran recovery with no reconciliation-capable adapter, and failed if `deliver` was called, the entry did not remain pending, or retry budget was consumed.

```text
info: Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
warn: Delivery entry <redacted-queue-id> delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation
warn: Retry failed for delivery <redacted-queue-id>: delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation
info: Delivery recovery complete: 0 recovered, 1 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
proof summary: {"recovered":0,"failed":1,"skippedMaxRetries":0,"deferredBackoff":0}
deliver call count: 0
pending entries: 1
queue row: {"status":"pending","retry_count":0,"recovery_state":"unknown_after_send","last_error":"delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation"}
```

Observed result after fix: recovery refused blind replay, did not call the delivery function, kept exactly one pending queue entry, and persisted `status: "pending"`, `retry_count: 0`, and `recovery_state: "unknown_after_send"`.

What was not tested: no live Discord gateway/channel send was performed in this pass. The broader transcript-to-provider delivery ledger, Discord reconciliation semantics, and Control UI/API delivery-state surface remain open under #87544.
