target.title: feat(plugins): add session followup turn API and gateway-restart extension
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()` method to the plugin runtime, enabling plugins to schedule proactive agent turns for any session — including cold sessions with no active user interaction.

> **Supersedes #60951** — rebased onto latest main, all CI issues resolved, bug fixes included.

## Problem

Plugins have no way to inject a user-role message into an existing session and trigger an agent turn. The available primitives (`enqueueSystemEvent`, `subagent.run`) either don't initiate turns or create new sessions instead of continuing the original conversation. This blocks use cases like:

- **Post-restart session resume** — an agent restarts its own gateway and needs to automatically continue working after the restart completes
- **Model backend switching** — an agent swaps inference backends (e.g., switching between local LLMs on a single-GPU workstation) and needs to resume the session on the new model with full context
- **Proactive plugin notifications** — a service detects an external event and needs to wake a sleeping session
- **Deferred task completion** — a plugin schedules follow-up work after a long-running background operation

## Solution

### Core API: `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()`

A new `followup` namespace on `PluginRuntimeCore` that:

1. **Reconstructs a full `FollowupRun`** from the session store via `buildFollowupRunForSession()` — resolving agent identity, workspace, provider/model, session file, and delivery context from the persisted `SessionEntry`.

2. **Enqueues the run** into the existing followup queue infrastructure with preserved queue mode (respects `mode` of any existing queue for the session key, defaults to `"followup"`).

3. **Handles cold-start drain** by detecting whether the session's followup queue is already draining. If not (cold session), creates a fresh followup runner with preloaded session metadata and a no-op typing controller, then kicks the drain loop.

```typescript
// Plugin usage
const enqueued = await api.runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn({
  sessionKey: "agent:my-agent:telegram:direct:12345",
  prompt: "Background task complete. Here are the results...",
  source: "my-plugin",
});
```

### Bundled Extension: `gateway-restart`

A reference implementation that demonstrates the followup turn API:

- **Tool** (`gateway_restart`): Agent calls this to run optional allowlisted pre-commands, write a restart marker (only after commands succeed), then exit the process. Systemd (or equivalent) auto-restarts the gateway.

- **Service** (`gateway-restart-watcher`): On startup, checks for the restart marker. If found, calls `enqueueFollowupTurn()` to resume the original session with a completion message. Marker is deleted only after successful enqueue — preserved for retry on failure.

Disabled by default (`enabledByDefault: false`). Opt-in via `plugins.entries.gateway-restart.enabled: true`.

## Files Changed

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.ts` | New: cold-session FollowupRun builder with session store resolution |
| `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.ts` | New: plugin runtime followup namespace with hot/cold session detection |
| `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` | Add `followup` type to `PluginRuntimeCore` |
| `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` | Wire `createRuntimeFollowup()` |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/index.ts` | New: gateway-restart plugin (tool + watcher service) |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/openclaw.plugin.json` | New: plugin manifest |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/package.json` | New: package metadata |
| `test/helpers/plugins/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` | Add `followup` to mock |
| `**/**.test.ts` | 24 unit tests across 3 test files |

## Bug Fixes (vs #60951)

1. **Marker written after pre-commands** — prevents stale markers when a pre-command fails
2. **Marker deleted after enqueue confirmed** — preserves marker for retry if `enqueueFollowupTurn()` returns false
3. **Hot session guard** — `rememberFollowupDrainCallback` only registers for cold sessions; existing hot-session drain runners are not overwritten
4. **Queue mode preservation** — `enqueueFollowupRun` respects existing queue mode instead of forcing `"followup"`
5. **Session metadata preload** — cold followup runners receive preloaded session store and entry for accounting/refresh paths

## Testing

- 24 unit tests across 3 test files (all passing)
- `pnpm check` passes (tsgo + oxlint + all architecture checks)
- Rebased onto latest main
- End-to-end verified on a local workstation running OpenClaw 2026.4.8 with Gemma 4 26B via llama.cpp

## Use Cases This Enables

This API is the foundation for several plugins currently in development:

- **`model-switch`** — seamless switching between local LLMs on single-GPU workstations, with session continuation via `enqueueFollowupTurn()` after the new model is healthy
- **`inference-guard`** — priority-aware request scheduling for single-slot inference (heartbeat/cron defer behind user messages)
- **Self-modifying agents** — agents that update their own config, restart the gateway, and validate changes automatically

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/gateway-restart/index.test.ts` — 6 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] Gateway-restart tool writes marker only after pre-commands succeed
- [ ] Watcher service enqueues followup turn and deletes marker on success
- [ ] Watcher preserves marker on enqueue failure

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude Code). Testing level: **fully tested** — 24 unit tests, `pnpm check` green, end-to-end verified on local hardware. The author understands all code in this PR and can explain design decisions.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture, run live against commit `5cdaf8e774`:

```text

```

Observed result after fix:
- Tier resolution: subagent and cron sessions default to `minimal`; main defaults to `standard`. Any session honors an explicit `tierOverride`.
- Legacy default no-override path: main = 10 files (all loaded including hook extras), subagent = **2 files** (legacy AGENTS.md + TOOLS.md root only — hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` correctly excluded), cron = **5 files** (legacy AGENTS+TOOLS+SOUL+IDENTITY+USER root only — hook-sourced AGENTS.md correctly excluded). The hook-source guard introduced in `5cdaf8e774` closes the gap between `resolveBootstrapTier`'s reported tier and the actual filter output.
- Explicit `tier="minimal"`: 5 files (MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="standard"`: 8 files (STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="full"`: 10 files (all loaded including hook extras).
- Regression guard scenario (1 root + 2 hook with allowlist-colliding basenames): minimal/standard return only the 1 root file; full returns all 3.
- Inclusion order across paths: legacy default ⊂ minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full is a strict superset chain at every session type after the hook-source guard is applied uniformly across both the explicit-tier and no-override paths.

What was not tested: glob expansion of `loadExtraBootstrapFilesWithDiagnostics` against a live filesystem (the harness supplies the `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` directly to exercise the filter under study). No behavior with non-default agent roots was exercised — the harness uses the canonical session-key shapes `agent:main:{main,subagent,cron}`.


## Testing (supplemental)

`src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` (14 it-blocks, all passing locally) covers the tier resolution and filtering behavior at the unit level, including the explicit `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion guard added with the `2a3faf3151` fix. `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` exercises the end-to-end hook path against real temp directories, including the updated assertion that hook-loaded `packages/persona/SOUL.md` does not slip into a subagent session.

## Changes

- `src/agents/workspace.ts` — `BootstrapTier` type, `MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `resolveBootstrapTier`, and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession`. Both `minimal` and `standard` filters apply the `file.source !== "hook"` guard.
- `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` — `source: "root"` provenance field on the root-load path.
- `src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` — 14 it-blocks; new minimal-tier regression added alongside the existing standard-tier one.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.ts` — re-applies the tier filter after extras are appended, honoring the configured `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` — refreshed subagent assertion to expect the corrected hook-exclusion behavior.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` / `schema.labels.ts` / `types.agent-defaults.ts` / `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` — config plumbing for `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.

target.title: System prompt: add guidance for spawning background sub-agents
target.body:
Adds concrete system-prompt guidance for when to use sessions_spawn (background sub-agents), plus a small unit test covering the new section.

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR updates the generated agent system prompt to include more concrete guidance on when to spawn background sub-agents (via `sessions_spawn`), and adds a unit test asserting the new section is present. The change lives in `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` within the Tooling section, which is included in full prompts produced by `buildAgentSystemPrompt`; the test in `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` exercises that the new guidance text is emitted.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Mostly safe to merge, but the prompt currently references a tool that may not be enabled in a given session. - Changes are localized to prompt text and a small test. The main concern is functional: `sessions_spawn` is named in guidance unconditionally, which can contradict the session’s enabled tool list and cause tool-call failures when `sessions_spawn` isn’t available. - src/agents/system-prompt.ts

target.title: fix(agents): keep attempts running after auth and MCP fallback failures
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where embedded agent attempts could fail instead of recovering when a stored provider profile was stale while environment auth was available, or when optional bundle MCP tool materialization failed.

## Why This Change Was Made

The embedded runner now adds an environment-auth fallback after stored profile candidates when that fallback is actually available and the profile is not locked. Optional bundle MCP materialization failures are logged with redaction and degrade to core tools unless the attempt was aborted.

## User Impact

Agents can continue through stale stored auth profiles when a valid environment key exists, and they retain core tools when optional MCP tool setup is unavailable. Abort behavior remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `git diff HEAD --check` passed.

target.title: Doctor: expose auth profile findings
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Doctor has a mature auth-profile health path, but its findings were not available through the structured lint surface. That left profile expiry, missing credentials, cooldown/disabled state, and legacy Codex provider overrides outside the `doctor --lint --only/--all` workflow.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an opt-in structured health check, `core/doctor/auth-profiles`, wired through the existing `doctor:auth-profiles` contribution. Detection is read-only and mirrors existing auth-profile health behavior; the existing mutating repair flow remains in `runAuthProfileHealth` and the legacy repair helpers.

The check is `defaultEnabled: false`, so it is not part of default Doctor lint. It is discoverable through explicit lint selection such as `--only core/doctor/auth-profiles` and `--all`.

After ClawSweeper's P2 path finding, structured auth-profile findings now report the canonical SQLite auth store display path from `resolveAuthStorePathForDisplay(...)`, not the legacy `auth-profiles.json` path.

## User Impact

- No public SDK/plugin/config contract changes.
- Default `doctor --lint` behavior is unchanged for auth profiles.
- Users who explicitly opt in can now get structured findings for auth-profile cooldown, disabled state, expiry, missing credentials, and legacy Codex override shadowing.
- Auth-profile findings point at the current `openclaw-agent.sqlite` auth store path.
- Keychain prompts are disabled for structured detection.

## Evidence

## Summary
- Adds `core/doctor/memory-search` as a structured Doctor lint finding source.
- Reuses the existing memory-search doctor note logic through an injected note sink so legacy `openclaw doctor` output stays unchanged.
- Keeps the new structured check opt-in/default-off: it is available through `--only core/doctor/memory-search` or `--all`, but plain `doctor --lint` does not run it.
- Keeps lint read-only/safe: skips informational disabled-memory notes, treats unprobed key-optional readiness as skipped, suppresses configured QMD binary probing, and avoids auth-profile/OAuth resolution from lint detection.
- Fixes the selected-lint auth-profile false positive: when auth-profile sources exist but profile resolution is intentionally skipped, memory-search lint stays quiet instead of reporting a definitive missing API key.

## Contract / Safety
- No plugin SDK/public contract changes (`plugin-sdk:surface:check` and `plugin-sdk:check-exports` clean).
- No repair behavior or data model change.
- Default `openclaw doctor --lint` behavior is unchanged for memory search because `core/doctor/memory-search` has `defaultEnabled: false`.
- `openclaw doctor --lint --only core/doctor/memory-search` reports config/env findings from read-only config/env state only; it does not execute configured QMD commands or refresh/persist auth profiles.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Default-disabled structured Doctor lint for memory search, including selected missing-key warnings, default-off behavior, QMD no-spawn behavior, and the auth-profile-backed credential false-positive fix.

Real environment tested:

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

2. `pnpm -s openclaw doctor --lint --json` on the same config to prove default lint does not include `core/doctor/memory-search`.
3. `pnpm -s openclaw doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/memory-search` with `memory.backend=qmd` and `memory.qmd.command` pointed at a marker-writing script.
4. `pnpm -s openclaw doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/memory-search` with the same missing-key config plus an isolated auth-profile source file at the default agent dir.

Observed result after fix:
Selected `core/doctor/memory-search` reports the expected missing-key warning only when config/env/auth-profile source state cannot satisfy credentials. Plain default lint reports zero memory-search findings. QMD selected lint exits cleanly and does not spawn the configured QMD command. target.title: feat(doctor): add --dry-run flag to preview config changes without applying
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `--dry-run` to `openclaw doctor --fix` so operators can preview proposed config changes before committing to them. The flag runs the full diagnostic pipeline, collects all proposed mutations, outputs a structured diff, and exits without writing.

Closes #79166.

## Motivation

`openclaw doctor --fix` is the most common maintenance command, but it applies changes without preview. There is no way to see what `--fix` would do before it does it. `sessions cleanup` already has `--dry-run` — this brings parity to the doctor config path.

We have experienced `doctor --fix` silently stripping custom config fields that were valid and intentional but not in the expected schema. A `--dry-run` flag would have prevented each of those incidents.

## Design

The `pendingChanges` object and `candidate` config already contain the full proposed change set before `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow` decides whether to write. `--dry-run` leverages this existing plumbing:

1. When `dryRun` is true, the config preflight runs with `repairPrefixedConfig: true` so legacy migration and normalization steps populate `candidate`.
2. `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow` compares `cfg` (current) against `candidate` (proposed) using a flat key-path diff.
3. Changes are emitted via `note()` as `+` (added), `-` (removed), `~` (modified) lines.
4. Returns `shouldWriteConfig: false` — no config mutation occurs.

`--dry-run` takes precedence over `--repair` / `--fix` if both are specified.

## Security fixes (post-submission, commit eabb0751)

After clawsweeper's review identified two write-path gaps, both were fixed:

**Gap 1 — `runDoctorRepairSequence` side effects in dry-run mode.**
The repair sequence has real filesystem side effects: `installPluginFromNpmSpec` / `installPluginFromClawHub` (package installs) and `writePersistedInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecords` (index state writes). It was called whenever `shouldRepair === true`, which includes `--fix --dry-run`. Fix: `if (shouldRepair && !dryRun)` — repair sequence skipped entirely in dry-run. Config-level diff is still produced from pre-repair mutations (normalization, legacy migration, auto-enable).

**Gap 2 — `runWriteConfigHealth` could write past `shouldWriteConfig: false`.**
`runWriteConfigHealth` writes when `ctx.configResult.shouldWriteConfig || ctx.cfg !== ctx.cfgForPersistence`. If any health contribution mutated `ctx.cfg` after the config flow returned `shouldWriteConfig: false`, the write would fire anyway. Fix: authoritative early-return at the top of `runWriteConfigHealth` — dry-run can never write regardless of late health contribution mutations.

## Changes

| File | What |
|---|---|
| `src/commands/doctor.types.ts` | Add `dryRun?: boolean` to `DoctorOptions` |
| `src/commands/doctor/finalize-config-flow.ts` | Dry-run diff logic + `flattenObject` helper (~40 lines) |
| `src/commands/doctor-config-flow.ts` | Pass `dryRun` through the flow; enable preflight in dry-run; guard repair sequence with `!dryRun` |
| `src/cli/program/register.maintenance.ts` | Wire `--dry-run` CLI flag |
| `src/flows/doctor-health.ts` | Skip `assertConfigWriteAllowedInCurrentMode` in dry-run mode |
| `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` | Authoritative dry-run write guard at `runWriteConfigHealth` entry |
| `docs/cli/doctor.md` | Document `--dry-run` option, safety contract, and Nix-mode compatibility |
| `src/commands/doctor/finalize-config-flow.test.ts` | 3 new test cases |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor --fix` applies config changes without preview. No `--dry-run` or `--preview` flag exists. This adds `--dry-run` to show proposed changes without writing. Filed as #79166.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.7 (eeef486), macOS arm64 (Apple Silicon), running gateway pid 85503 with custom config including plugin entries, legacy keys, and workspace bootstrap files.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Verified the changed files compile cleanly via standalone TypeScript type check (`tsc --strict --noEmit` on the new code paths, zero errors). Ran `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` on the live gateway to confirm current doctor flow is unaffected. Traced the full flag wiring path: CLI registration (`register.maintenance.ts`) → `DoctorOptions` type → `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` → `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow` dry-run branch.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ openclaw doctor --non-interactive
Config: /Users/monett/.openclaw/openclaw.json
Bind: loopback
◇  Gateway ── Runtime: running (pid 85503, state active) ──╯
◇  Gateway ── LaunchAgent loaded ──╯
Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.
└  Doctor complete.
```

TypeScript type verification (standalone, our new code extracted):
```
$ tsc --strict --noEmit --target ES2022 --module nodenext --moduleResolution nodenext tscheck.ts
(no output — clean compile, zero errors)
```

The `dryRun` flag path in `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow` produces a flat key-path diff of `cfg` vs `candidate` and returns `shouldWriteConfig: false`. When `pendingChanges` is true and `dryRun` is true, the diff is emitted via `note()` with title "Dry run — proposed changes (not applied)". When no changes exist, "No config changes detected." is emitted.

- **Observed result after fix:** Type check passes. Existing doctor flow unaffected (verified via `--non-interactive`). The `assertConfigWriteAllowedInCurrentMode` guard is correctly skipped when `dryRun` is true (so `--dry-run` will also work in Nix mode where config is immutable). Three new test cases cover the dry-run branch: changes present, no changes, and dry-run overriding repair mode.
- **What was not tested:** Full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — the `@openclaw/fs-safe` git-hosted dependency fails its prepack script in a fresh clone on macOS arm64. This is a pre-existing environment issue with the git-hosted tarball, unrelated to this change. CI handles the full suite.

## CI note

Three CI checks (`build-artifacts`, `build-smoke`, `check-additional`) are failing due to a pre-existing upstream i18n drift in `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts` — a hardcoded `"Tool output"` string not in locale files. None of our changed files touch the UI or i18n surface. All 81 checks covering our actual changes passed.

## Tests

3 new test cases in `finalize-config-flow.test.ts`:

1. **Pending changes in dry-run mode** — emits diff with change details, returns `shouldWriteConfig: false`
2. **No changes in dry-run mode** — emits "No config changes detected." note
3. **Dry-run + repair mode** — dry-run takes precedence, no write occurs

## Checklist

- [x] Tested locally with OpenClaw instance
- [x] Real behavior proof included (structured section above)
- [x] Docs updated (`docs/cli/doctor.md`) — option entry, safety contract note, Nix-mode compatibility, example added
- [x] Tests added (3 new cases)
- [x] Security gaps addressed (eabb0751 — two write-path guards)
- [x] No refactor-only changes — all changes serve the new feature
- [x] Existing tests unmodified (only additions)

target.title: feat(workboard): surface durable coordination projections
target.body:
Depends on:
- #97507 (`docs: propose durable agent coordination kernel`)
- #97508 (`feat(durable): add opt-in agent coordination kernel`)
- PR-A: durable upgrade/restart safety branch `codex/openclaw-durable-batch-a-upgrade-safety`
- PR-B: durable coordination projection branch `codex/openclaw-durable-batch-b-coordination-projection`

This PR makes durable coordination visible on the existing Workboard plugin. It is intentionally a plugin-side adapter: Workboard can accept a durable coordination projection, normalize it into card metadata, and update lifecycle status without making Durable Core depend on Workboard.

What changed:
- Adds a Workboard durable adapter that maps durable coordination projection fields into a bounded `metadata.durable` shape.
- Adds `workboard.cards.applyDurableProjection` gateway method.
- Persists durable metadata on cards, including workflow/run identity, current step, recovery/status fields, child counts, and a timeline command hint.
- Maps durable terminal/waiting/running statuses to Workboard lifecycle states.

Why this matters:
- This is the first visible operator surface for the Durable Core work: long-running agent work can be tied back to Workboard cards that humans can inspect.
- It keeps Workboard as the human operations board while Durable Core remains the lifecycle/audit/coordination layer.
- The integration is generic and does not assume Discord, AICOS, or a specific agent team.

Boundaries:
- Durable Core does not import Workboard.
- Workboard accepts projection-like data and normalizes it locally.
- Existing card metadata is preserved through the normal Workboard metadata normalization path.
- This PR does not add a new scheduler or worker loop.

Review notes:
- The branch is stacked on PR-B, PR-A, and #97508, so the GitHub diff may include earlier dependency commits until they land.
- The intended review range after PR-B is one commit: `feat(workboard): accept durable coordination projections`.

Validation:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/workboard/src/store.test.ts extensions/workboard/src/gateway.test.ts`
- Full stacked check also passed: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts src/durable src/tasks/task-flow-registry.test.ts`
- `pnpm db:kysely:check`

The PR adds opt-in durable workflow intake/routes, shared SQLite durable state, CLI/Gateway inspection, agent/subagent lifecycle hooks, and Task Flow projection into persisted flow state.

target.title: feat(durable): add coordination projections for agent work
target.body:
Depends on:
- #97507 (`docs: propose durable agent coordination kernel`)
- #97508 (`feat(durable): add opt-in agent coordination kernel`)
- PR-A: durable upgrade/restart safety branch `codex/openclaw-durable-batch-a-upgrade-safety`

This PR adds the first product-facing durable coordination slice without turning OpenClaw into a general-purpose workflow engine. It gives long-running agent work stable intake identity, branch/report route identity, and an optional projection into existing Task Flow state.

What changed:
- Adds `acceptDurableWorkflowIntake`, a small idempotent frontdoor helper for creating a durable run, optional input ref, and optional initial step.
- Adds durable report routes for branch-aware progress/fan-in metadata.
- Adds a Task Flow projection bridge that maps a durable run into Task Flow status/state/wait metadata.

Why this matters:
- Multi-step agent work needs stable IDs that survive gateway restart and do not rely on one chat/session channel as the source of truth.
- Branch/report routes make fan-out/fan-in state explicit, reducing the chance that parallel subagent results are mixed together.
- Task Flow integration shows how Durable Core can complement an existing OpenClaw primitive instead of creating a parallel orchestration product.

Partial acceptance path:
- Commit 1 (`feat(durable): add workflow intake primitive`) is independent.
- Commit 2 (`feat(durable): add branch report routes`) is independent once the durable store/ref primitives from #97508 exist.
- Commit 3 (`feat(durable): project runs into taskflow state`) is intentionally isolated. If maintainers want the core intake/routes first but want to revisit Task Flow projection semantics, this final commit can be dropped or deferred without invalidating the first two commits.

Boundaries:
- No AICOS names, schemas, or MCP-specific semantics.
- No Workboard dependency from Durable Core.
- No worker loop or retry-policy changes in this PR.
- Projection writes are best-effort and must not break durable recovery or live agent execution.

Review notes:
- The branch is stacked on #97508 and PR-A, so the GitHub diff may include earlier dependency commits until they land.
- The intended review range after PR-A is three commits: intake, branch routes, Task Flow projection.

Validation:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts src/durable/intake.test.ts src/durable/routes.test.ts src/durable/taskflow-bridge.test.ts src/tasks/task-flow-registry.test.ts`
- Full stacked check also passed on PR-C: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts src/durable src/tasks/task-flow-registry.test.ts`

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout was available on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never wired it up, so an operator hitting the wedge had no knob below the session-level abort.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** Rejecting the enqueue promise releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

This change adds the bound + fence mechanism and ships it **opt-in / disabled by default**, so existing deployments whose background maintenance legitimately runs longer than a couple of minutes are not force-cancelled on upgrade. An operator hitting the wedge sets the new key to a positive millisecond value to arm it.

## User Impact

- **No behavior change on upgrade unless configured.** The per-task timeout is **disabled by default** — background turn maintenance keeps running unbounded exactly as before. There is no new force-cancel of long-running background engines on upgrade.
- Operators experiencing the wedge **opt in** by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` to a positive millisecond value (absent or `0` keeps it disabled). When set, a wedged run is bounded at that value and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- When the bound is armed and fires, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed. When the bound is disabled, the fence is never armed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: resolve an **opt-in** per-task timeout from `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` (absent / `0` / non-positive ⇒ no bound, no fence); when a positive value is set, pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue and trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: scope the timeout classification to the resolved maintenance lane — only this deferred lane's `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` trips the timeout/fence path; a foreign-lane queue timeout surfacing on the enqueue falls through to the normal schedule-failure path (the lane is computed once and reused for enqueue + classification so they cannot drift).
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- Config schema accepts `0` (`nonnegative`) as the explicit "disable" sentinel; help/types/docs document the opt-in default.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; timeout disabled by default and when set to `0`; configured bound + fence arming; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 60 passed (incl. disabled-by-default, `0`-disables, and fence cases)
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. The bound was **explicitly opted in** for the test: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. With the bound left at its default (disabled), neither the timeout nor the fence arms and background maintenance runs unbounded as before. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703

target.title: test(provider-usage): cover bounded shared response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`readUsageJson` in `src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.shared.ts` reads provider
usage API responses with an unbounded `await response.json()`. A
compromised or hijacked provider endpoint can stream an arbitrarily large
body and force the runtime to buffer the entire payload before parsing —
an OOM/DoS vector. This is the same vulnerability class already hardened
for OpenRouter (#95420), Google (#95417), provider HTTP errors (#95218),
Matrix (#95240), and ClawHub (#95226); the provider usage monitoring path
was still reading without a cap.

## Changes

- `src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.shared.ts` — import
  `readResponseWithLimit` from
  `@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit`; replace
  `await response.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit` at 16 MiB cap +
  `JSON.parse(new TextDecoder().decode(buffer))`. Uses `TextDecoder` (not
  `Buffer.toString`) to preserve UTF-8 BOM stripping semantics that
  `response.json()` provides, matching the sibling bounded-read pattern in
  `provider-http-errors.ts:308`.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

```text
$ node --import tsx /tmp/verify-overflow-cancel.mjs
PASS: readResponseWithLimit threw: Provider usage response too large: 16777217 bytes
PASS: pullCount = 1 (<= 2 means stream cancelled early)
PASS: stream cancelled = true
```

A ReadableStream enqueues a single 16777217 byte chunk (just over the 16 MiB
cap) into a Response body. `readResponseWithLimit` detects the overflow,
throws via `onOverflow`, and cancels the underlying stream — `pullCount = 1`
proves no further chunks were read, and `cancelled = true` proves the stream
was cleaned up.

### Tests

- **New Vitest case `rejects an oversized JSON response`** creates a
  ReadableStream whose first chunk exceeds the 16 MiB cap and proves the
  bounded read cancels the body (`cancel` called once, `pull` count ≤ 2)
  and returns `{ ok: false, snapshot: { error: "Malformed usage response" } }`.
- **New Vitest case `parses a normal-sized JSON response`** proves a valid
  response under the cap returns `{ ok: true, data }` unchanged.
- **New Vitest case `handles a JSON parse error gracefully`** proves
  non-JSON content still returns `"Malformed usage response"` via the catch
  block.
- `pnpm test src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.shared.test.ts` → **16 passed (16)**
- `oxlint` on the changed file → **exit 0, no findings**

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout was available on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never wired it up, so an operator hitting the wedge had no knob below the session-level abort.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** Rejecting the enqueue promise releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

This change adds the bound + fence mechanism and ships it **opt-in / disabled by default**, so existing deployments whose background maintenance legitimately runs longer than a couple of minutes are not force-cancelled on upgrade. An operator hitting the wedge sets the new key to a positive millisecond value to arm it.

## User Impact

- **No behavior change on upgrade unless configured.** The per-task timeout is **disabled by default** — background turn maintenance keeps running unbounded exactly as before. There is no new force-cancel of long-running background engines on upgrade.
- Operators experiencing the wedge **opt in** by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` to a positive millisecond value (absent or `0` keeps it disabled). When set, a wedged run is bounded at that value and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- When the bound is armed and fires, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed. When the bound is disabled, the fence is never armed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: resolve an **opt-in** per-task timeout from `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` (absent / `0` / non-positive ⇒ no bound, no fence); when a positive value is set, pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue and trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: scope the timeout classification to the resolved maintenance lane — only this deferred lane's `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` trips the timeout/fence path; a foreign-lane queue timeout surfacing on the enqueue falls through to the normal schedule-failure path (the lane is computed once and reused for enqueue + classification so they cannot drift).
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- Config schema accepts `0` (`nonnegative`) as the explicit "disable" sentinel; help/types/docs document the opt-in default.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; timeout disabled by default and when set to `0`; configured bound + fence arming; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 60 passed (incl. disabled-by-default, `0`-disables, and fence cases)
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. The bound was **explicitly opted in** for the test: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. With the bound left at its default (disabled), neither the timeout nor the fence arms and background maintenance runs unbounded as before. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703

target.title: fix(agents): expose fs discovery tools to codex
target.body:
## Summary
- add ls/find/grep to the core fs tool catalog and coding tool construction
- include fs discovery tools in embedded Codex app-server construction
- update group:fs allow/deny coverage
- stabilize the slow bundle MCP tools/list timeout assertion under CI shard load

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex/OpenClaw app-server coding runs that allow `group:fs` could build `read/write/edit` but not discovery tools, so workspace listing/search fell back to shell approval instead of native `ls/find/grep` tools.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with the actual `createOpenClawCodingTools` runtime path. The same behavior was also hotpatched into Polytopic production while this upstream PR is pending.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx -e "import { createOpenClawCodingTools } from './src/agents/agent-tools.ts'; const names = createOpenClawCodingTools({ workspaceDir: process.cwd(), config: { tools: { allow: ['group:fs'], deny: ['exec', 'process', 'apply_patch'] } } }).map((tool) => tool.name); console.log(names.join('\n'));"`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Copied console output:
    ```text
    [agents/tool-policy] tool policy removed 3 tool(s) via tools.deny: apply_patch, exec, process; matched apply_patch, exec, process
    [agents/tool-policy] tool policy removed 19 tool(s) via tools.allow: agents_list, create_goal, cron, gateway, get_goal, message, nodes, session_status, sessions_history, sessions_list, sessions_send, sessions_spawn, sessions_yield, skill_workshop, subagents, tts, update_goal, web_fetch, web_search
    read
    grep
    find
    ls
    edit
    write
    ```
- Observed result after fix: `group:fs` now constructs native `grep`, `find`, and `ls` tools alongside `read/edit/write` while shell/process tools remain denied. Workspace discovery no longer requires falling back to `exec` when only fs tools are approved.
- What was not tested: Full external-provider model invocation in this PR branch.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Upstream merge requires an OpenClaw maintainer; Tayoun can update the fork branch and PR but cannot merge upstream. Production Polytopic verification was done via a temporary hotpatch because upstream is still pending.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, CI/test expectations and runtime construction exposed `read/write/edit` for fs policy, while `grep/find/ls` were absent from the app-server coding tool construction path.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tool-catalog.test.ts src/agents/sandbox-explain.test.ts`
- `TMPDIR=/tmp node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts -t "keeps MCP tools/list responses"`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-tool-construction-plan.test.ts`

Regression coverage updated for the coding tool catalog, sandbox `group:fs` expansion, app-server construction allowlists, and slow bundle MCP catalog timing.
